macro_segments.title: Two people colliding in the public domain with equal rights and mutual participation

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: barrel and beam colliding head-on in reshus harabim
Principle of adam muad l'olam offset by peshiat hanizak when both are negligent
Case of one leading and one following: liability depends on whose action caused the accident
Standing still versus signaling: distinction in causation of damage
Reversed order: one with barrel leading, one with beam following

macro_segments.title: Intercourse and shared domain cases examining when both parties are active

micro_segments.title: Gemara challenges: husband injuring wife during intercourse—is both having permission enough to exempt?
Rava's kal vachomer from forest (yaar) case: negligence applies even with permission
Gemara response: distinguishing by active versus passive role; man is actor in intercourse
Clarification: both parameters matter—mutual action and mutual permission needed for exemption

macro_segments.title: Cows crouching versus walking: non-permission as basis for liability

micro_segments.title: Reish Lakish: crouching cow has no right; walking cow kicking it is exempt
Gemara attempts to support Reish Lakish from mishnah's second case
Gemara distinguishes: proof only works if beam went horizontal; animals avoid obstacles

macro_segments.title: Running in the public domain and permission contingent on circumstance

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: one running, one walking, or both running—both exempt from liability
Isi ben Yehuda disputes: only ערב שבת בין השמשות permits running; otherwise runner is liable
Rav Yochanan rules like Isi ben Yehuda; gemara reconciles with mishnah
Why erev Shabbos permits running: need to prepare for Shabbos observance
Kabbalistic development: Kabalat Shabbos from Rav Chanina and Rabbi Yannai's practices

macro_segments.title: Chopping wood: damages in different combinations of domains

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: chopping in reshus harabim or reshus hayachid with damage in either domain—all liable
Gemara explains necessity of all three cases to cover combinations of permission and location

macro_segments.title: Carpenter's workshop: permission versus non-permission and implications for galus

micro_segments.title: Baraita: one entering without permission exempt from damages; with permission liable
Rabbi Yose bar Chanina: liable for four payments but exempt from galus
Rabi Yose distinguishes carpenter case from yaar case: not the domain question but negligence
Rava/Rabbah: kal vachomer—here more awareness than yaar yet still exempt from galus
Rava reinterprets: negligence too high, approaching mezid, hence no atonement in galus
Objection from extra lash case: even with high negligence, galus is granted
Resolution: judge miscounted; lasher unaware of error, not in shogeg karov l'mezid category
Further objection from stone-thrower in reshus harabim with high negligence still receives galus
Rav Shmuel bar Yitzchak: knocking down wall at night in dump—no negligence if no foot traffic
Rav Pappa: dump used for bathroom at night but not day; neither mezid nor ones
Alternative reading: baraita addresses galus not nezek; without permission still chayav four payments
Resolution: ברייתא Baraita addresses only galus; patur in galus without reshus, chayav with reshus
